diagrams.title: Memory Model: Variable and Pointer Storage
diagrams.description: A visual representation showing how a regular variable and a pointer variable are stored in memory with their respective addresses and values
elements: Memory boxes labeled with addresses (e.g., 0x1000, 0x1004, 0x1008)
Regular variable 'int num = 25' stored at address 0x1000 with value 25 shown inside
Pointer variable 'int *ptr' stored at address 0x1004 showing value 0x1000 (the address of num)
Arrow from pointer value 0x1000 pointing to num's memory location
Labels: 'Variable Name: num', 'Address: 0x1000', 'Value: 25' for regular variable
Labels: 'Pointer Name: ptr', 'Address: 0x1004', 'Value: 0x1000 (address of num)' for pointer
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how pointers store memory addresses rather than actual values, and understand the distinction between a variable's address and its stored value

diagrams.title: Pointer Declaration and Initialization Process
diagrams.description: Step-by-step flow diagram showing how to declare a pointer and assign it a variable's address
elements: Rectangle boxes representing steps: (1) Declare pointer: int *ptr; (2) Declare variable: int num = 25; (3) Initialize pointer: ptr = #
Arrows connecting steps in sequence
Memory visualization after each step showing empty pointer box, then variable box with 25, then pointer box containing 0x1000
Code snippets displayed inside each step box
Highlighting of & operator in 'ptr = &num' showing it means 'address of num'
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete workflow of creating a pointer, declaring a variable, and connecting them using the address-of operator

diagrams.title: Dereferencing: Accessing Value Through Pointer
diagrams.description: Visual demonstration of the dereference operator (*) and how it retrieves or modifies the value at the pointed-to address
elements: Two-part diagram: (a) Reading value: *ptr displays value 25
(b) Writing value: *ptr = 50 changes num to 50
Arrow from *ptr in code to the actual memory location of num
Before/After memory boxes showing change from 25 to 50
Code examples: printf("%d", *ptr) and *ptr = 50
Color coding: pointer location in blue, dereferenced value in green
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how to access and modify the original variable's value indirectly through the pointer using the dereference operator

diagrams.title: Relationship: & (Address-of) vs * (Dereference) Operators
diagrams.description: Comparative visual showing the two complementary operators used with pointers
elements: Left side: & operator diagram - arrow pointing FROM variable TO its address (e.g., &num → 0x1000)
Right side: * operator diagram - arrow pointing FROM pointer TO value (e.g., *ptr → 25)
Memory boxes showing before and after each operation
Truth table style: & returns address type, * returns value type
Mnemonic: '& gives address, * gives value'
Examples: printf("%p", &num) prints address, printf("%d", *ptr) prints value
diagrams.purpose: Students clearly distinguish between the address-of operator (&) which gets an address, and the dereference operator (*) which gets the value at an address

diagrams.title: Pointer to Different Data Types
diagrams.description: Diagram showing how pointers work with different data types (int, float, char, double) and their respective sizes
elements: Four memory layout examples side by side
int *ptr1 pointing to int (4 bytes)
float *ptr2 pointing to float (4 bytes)
char *ptr3 pointing to char (1 byte)
double *ptr4 pointing to double (8 bytes)
Address increments shown: ptr1+1 moves by 4 bytes, ptr3+1 moves by 1 byte
Note: Pointer type determines arithmetic behavior and interpretation of memory
diagrams.purpose: Students understand that pointers are type-specific and the data type affects pointer arithmetic and how bytes are interpreted

charts.title: & (Address-of) vs * (Dereference) Operator Comparison
charts.content: Operator | Symbol | Purpose | Returns | Example | Result | |--------|--------|---------|---------|---------|--------| | Address-of | & | Get memory address of variable | Address (pointer) | &num | 0x1000 | | Dereference | * | Get value at address | Value (original type) | *ptr | 25 | | Declaration | * | Declare pointer variable | Pointer type | int *ptr | Creates pointer |

charts.title: Steps to Use Pointers to Access Variables
charts.content: Step 1: Declare a variable (int num = 25) → Step 2: Declare a pointer (int *ptr) → Step 3: Initialize pointer with address (ptr = &num) → Step 4a: Read value through pointer (printf(*ptr)) → Step 4b: Modify value through pointer (*ptr = 50) → Both operations affect original variable

charts.title: Pointer Declaration Syntax Variations
charts.content: Syntax | Meaning | Notes | |--------|---------|-------| | int *ptr | Pointer to int | Recommended (clear that * belongs to ptr) | | int* ptr | Pointer to int | Valid but * seems attached to type | | int * ptr | Pointer to int | Valid but spacing is unusual | | int *ptr1, *ptr2 | Two pointers | Both are pointers | | int *ptr1, num | ptr1 is pointer, num is int | Only *ptr1 is pointer |

charts.title: Common Pointer Operations Summary
charts.content: Operation | Code | Description | Result | |------------------|-----------------|------------------------------|----------| | Get address | ptr = &num | Assign address of num to ptr | ptr = 0x1000 | | Get value | x = *ptr | Assign value at ptr to x | x = 25 | | Modify value | *ptr = 50 | Change value at ptr to 50 | num = 50 | | Pointer of pointer | **q = &ptr | q points to pointer ptr | **q = 25 |

branches: Declaration: int *ptr; or int* ptr; creates pointer variable
Initialization: ptr = # links pointer to variable's address
Dereferencing: *ptr accesses/updates the value at the address
Memory Model: Pointers store addresses, not values
Data Types: Pointer type must match variable type for proper interpretation
Pointer Arithmetic: ptr+n moves by sizeof(type) bytes
Applications: Dynamic memory, arrays, linked structures, function parameters
mindMaps.purpose: Complete conceptual overview connecting declaration, initialization, dereferencing, and practical applications - essential foundation for linked lists and dynamic data structures
